title: Namakku Naame: A Milestone in Stalin's Political Life!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. `Namakku Naame` is retained as the established campaign name; its literal sense, “We for Ourselves,” is supplied at its first explanatory occurrence. The Tamil reading copy corrects scan-confirmed OCR errors and broken word joins, including the Emergency year `1916` to `1976`, `ஸ்டாவின்` to `ஸ்டாலினை`, and `சொலலாகியிருக்கிறது` to `சொல்லாகியிருக்கிறது`. Highlighted passages repeated later in the printed source are preserved. The canonical Tamil source file is unchanged.
paragraphs: On the morning of 12 February 2016, my assistant Nithya came to me and said, “Thalapathi Anna is completing today his *Namakku Naame* journey through all 234 constituencies. As soon as the journey ends, he is coming to meet you in person and receive your blessings.” I hurriedly bathed, and I, the “Leader,” waited for the arrival of the “Treasurer”! At that moment, my attention and memory did not settle on the television set before me. Without even telling me, they had travelled back to 1976.

In 1976, the Emergency was proclaimed and members of the Kazhagam were hunted down under the MISA law. Not only members of the Kazhagam, but many others too were arrested and imprisoned: my younger brother K. Veeramani, now President of the Dravidar Kazhagam and then its General Secretary; Viduthalai Sambandam; Nadigavel M. R. Radha; members of the Marxist Communist Party; and members of the old Congress. To name just a few of those arrested in Chennai district, there were Murasoli Maran, Arcot Veerasamy, A. V. P. Asaithambi, Neelanarayanan, M. K. Stalin, Chittibabu, R. T. Seethapathi, T. R. Baalu, A. Selvarasan, Sa. Ganesan, So. Ma. Ramachandran, Pazhakadai Jayaraman, Thousand Lights Hussain and advocate R. Ganesan. It is a long list. As in Chennai, leading members of the Kazhagam in every other district were arrested and thrown into prison cells.

The Emergency was proclaimed in 1976, and members of the Kazhagam were hunted down under MISA. Stalin was arrested only a few months after his marriage. Prison officers surrounded him, and when we were allowed to see him, he was wearing a full-sleeved shirt that concealed his arms. On seeing me, he sat before me, suppressing the tears that brimmed in his eyes. “They say you were beaten. Is that true?” I asked. He merely shook his head to say, “No.” He was in such an emotional state that, had he opened his mouth to answer in words, he might have broken down and wept! It was that same Stalin whom I met at Gopalapuram on the morning of 12 February 2016.

In other prisons across the state, close relatives were permitted to see MISA detainees once a week. But in Chennai prison, neither parents nor relatives were allowed to meet them. Reports spread that all the leading figures were being subjected to brutal torture inside the prison, and the families of those Udanpirappugal came to me in tears. I personally telephoned the head of the police and announced that, unless the relatives were allowed to see the MISA detainees by the next day, I would begin a fast unto death at the prison gates. Only then was permission granted. That was how I went with my family to the prison to see Maran and Stalin. Only after we arrived there were we told that Maran had fallen gravely ill with convulsions and had been admitted to the Government General Hospital in Chennai. We were then permitted to see Stalin alone.

Stalin had been arrested only a few months after his marriage. Prison officers surrounded him, and when we were allowed to see him, he was wearing a full-sleeved shirt that concealed his arms. On seeing me, he sat before me, suppressing the tears that brimmed in his eyes. “They say you were beaten; is that true?” I asked. He merely shook his head to say, “No.” He was in such an emotional state that, had he opened his mouth to answer in words, he might have broken down and wept!

It was that same Stalin whom I met at Gopalapuram on the morning of 12 February 2016. Setting aside the memories that had until then played like shadows across my mind, I blessed Stalin with all my heart. Two hundred and thirty-four constituencies: the *Namakku Naame* Dawn Recovery Journey that Stalin began beside the Thiruvalluvar statue in Kanyakumari on 20 September 2015 concluded in T. Nagar on 12 February 2016. Over 146 days, apart from a few days when he attended other Kazhagam events, younger brother Stalin successfully travelled throughout Tamil Nadu, covering every Assembly constituency. He invited representatives of every section of Tamil society and heard their demands and grievances directly. He also visited areas affected by the great floods and rains, met and consoled the people there, and distributed relief assistance. One cannot but praise the extraordinary work done day and night, without fatigue, by all the district secretaries of the Kazhagam to make this journey a complete success. I know that no district secretary regarded the work as a burden or dwelt on its difficulties; they undertook it with immense happiness and enthusiasm. To put it another way, wherever Stalin went in body during that journey, his feelings were filled with my memories and thoughts.

The mothers who rejoiced on seeing him during the journey; the crowds who intercepted him on the road, shook his hand and celebrated; those generous people who, their faces beaming, invited him into their little tea shops and served him tea; the mothers who placed their children in Stalin's arms and delighted in seeing him embrace them warmly to his heart: none of them can be forgotten.

Even before the journey, Stalin came to me and said that he planned to undertake such a tour. He asked, “Appa! You must be the one to name this journey.” I could not sleep the whole night! I kept thinking about what name to give it. Then the words *Namakku Naame*—“We for Ourselves”—came to my mind. When Stalin met me the next morning, I wrote the name down and gave it to him. The journey was planned in three phases to cover all 234 Assembly constituencies. Over the past four months, he must have met nearly three crore people during this journey.

News of the *Namakku Naame* journey spread among the people of Tamil Nadu and began creating fear among the ruling party. At the same time, people in high places and senior government officials began exchanging news about *Namakku Naame*. Every morning during the journey, Stalin telephoned me and recounted all the important events of the previous day. On the one hand I was happy; on the other, I wondered anxiously whether his slender body could bear all this. But I never showed that worry outwardly.

It is no exaggeration to say that younger brother Stalin's *Namakku Naame* Dawn Recovery Journey through all 234 constituencies of Tamil Nadu has generated enthusiasm among our Kazhagam's Udanpirappugal, an awakening blended with hope among the people, goodwill among the neutral, bewilderment among our opponents, and constructive interest and expectation among the press and other media. The very name *Namakku Naame* has become a phrase inseparably woven into every household. In sum, Stalin's journey was a victorious journey; an innovative journey worthy of inscription in the history of the Kazhagam; a most important milestone in younger brother Stalin's political life!

The thought that he must meet the people directly and explain the rulers' wrongs to them may have come to younger brother Stalin now, but the same thought arose in me in those days too. On 26 November 1980, Subramania Pillai, an Assistant Commissioner of the Hindu Religious and Charitable Endowments Department, was murdered by trustees belonging to the ruling party at the lodge in the Tiruchendur temple complex. When an attempt was made to conceal the crime by calling it a suicide, the DMK raised its battle cry. Justice Paul, appointed to investigate, named the culprits in his report. Condemning the government's failure to act against them, I led Kazhagam comrades on a “Long March for Justice” from Madurai to Tiruchendur on 15 February 1982.

It was a journey of 200 kilometres from Madurai to Tiruchendur: eight days, eight public meetings and several lakh people encountered! By the morning of the third day, my feet were covered in blisters. My assistant, who was travelling with me, saw them and cried, urging me to end the march. I paid no heed. The doctors accompanying me treated the blisters all along the route as we continued. It was this memory that returned to me when younger brother Stalin spoke to me on the telephone.

It is no exaggeration to say that younger brother Stalin's *Namakku Naame* Dawn Recovery Journey through all 234 constituencies of Tamil Nadu has generated enthusiasm among our Kazhagam's Udanpirappugal, an awakening blended with hope among the people, goodwill among the neutral, bewilderment among our opponents, and constructive interest and expectation among the press and other media. The very name *Namakku Naame* has become a phrase inseparably woven into every household. In sum, Stalin's journey was a victorious journey; an innovative journey worthy of inscription in the history of the Kazhagam; a most important milestone in younger brother Stalin's political life!

The successive wrongs of the AIADMK rulers have certainly contributed to the present resurgence of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam. But it is no exaggeration to say that, because of this *Namakku Naame* journey undertaken by younger brother M. K. Stalin, people from every section have come forward to support the Kazhagam with affection and goodwill. At this moment, when his *Namakku Naame* journey through all 234 constituencies has concluded, I wholeheartedly commend and congratulate the Kazhagam Treasurer, younger brother M. K. Stalin, who has been both a cause of and a support for this resurgence of the Kazhagam.
